(**Lt Gen**, **LTG** and similar) is a [military](/wiki/Military "Military") rank used in many countries. The rank traces its origins to the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), where the title of lieutenant general was held by the [second-in-command](/wiki/Second-in-command "Second-in-command") on the battlefield, who was normally subordinate to a [captain general](/wiki/Captain_general "Captain general").

**Lieutenant general** (**Lt Gen**, **LTG** and similar) is a [military](/wiki/Military "Military") rank used in many countries. The rank traces its origins to the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), where the title of lieutenant general was held by the [second-in-command](/wiki/Second-in-command "Second-in-command") on the battlefield, who was normally subordinate to a [captain general](/wiki/Captain_general "Captain general").

In modern armies, lieutenant general normally ranks immediately below [general](/wiki/General "General") (or [colonel general](/wiki/Colonel_general "Colonel general")) and above [major general](/wiki/Major_general "Major general"); it is equivalent to the navy rank of [vice admiral](/wiki/Vice_admiral "Vice admiral"), and in air forces with a separate rank structure, it is equivalent to [air marshal](/wiki/Air_marshal "Air marshal"). In the United States, a lieutenant general has a three star insignia and commands an [army corps](/wiki/Army_corps "Army corps"), typically made up of three [army divisions](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)"), and consisting of around 60,000 to 70,000 soldiers.

The seeming incongruity that a lieutenant general outranks a major general (whereas a [major](/wiki/Major_(rank) "Major (rank)") outranks a [lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant")) is due to the derivation of major general from [sergeant major general](/wiki/Sergeant_major_general "Sergeant major general"), which was a rank subordinate to lieutenant general (as a lieutenant outranks a [sergeant major](/wiki/Sergeant_major "Sergeant major")). Several countries (e.g. Balkan states) use the rank of [lieutenant colonel general](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_general "Lieutenant colonel general") instead of lieutenant general, in an attempt to solve this apparent anomaly.[[1]](#cite_note-Albanian_Army-1)

In contrast, in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and a number of other countries of the former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), lieutenant general is a rank immediately below [colonel general](/wiki/Colonel_general "Colonel general"), and above [major general](/wiki/Major_general "Major general") – in these systems there is no use of the [brigadier general](/wiki/Brigadier_general "Brigadier general") of many Western countries.

In addition, some countries use the lieutenant general as the rank of divisional commander, and some have designated them with
[French revolutionary system](/wiki/General_officer#French_Revolutionary_system "General officer"). For example, some countries of South America use [divisional general](/wiki/Divisional_general "Divisional general") as the equivalent of lieutenant general.
